title: What are the names of the compliance for FDA (Food and Drug Administration)

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: What are the names of the compliance for FDA (Food and Drug Administration)

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The four key areas of FDA compliance are:

1. Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) - establishes minimum requirements for the manufacturing, processing, packaging, or holding of drugs, medical devices, and dietary supplements

2. Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) - ensures that food is safe by identifying and controlling physical, chemical, and biological hazards during production and processing

3. Labeling Compliance - ensures that food and drug products meet accurate and truthful labeling requirements to aid consumer understanding

4. Clinical Trials Compliance - ensures that clinical trials for drugs and medical devices are conducted ethically and the safety of subjects is protected.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: What are the names of the compliance act for FDA (Food and Drug Administration)

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The following are some of the acts that pertain to FDA compliance:

1. Food, Drug, and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act - the primary law overseeing all aspects of food and drug safety in the United States, including regulating ingredients, labeling, and packaging

2. Medical Device Amendments (MDA) - amended the FD&C Act and established additional regulatory requirements for the safety and effectiveness of medical devices

3. Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (DSHEA) - regulates dietary supplements and provides guidelines for labeling and advertising

4. Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA) - focuses on preventive measures to improve the safety of the U.S. food supply by shifting the focus from responding to contamination to preventing it

5. Drug Supply Chain Security Act (DSCSA) - requires enhanced security and tracking measures for prescription drugs as they move through the supply chain to reduce the risk of counterfeit or contaminated products.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: what is FDA (Food and Drug Administration)

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: The FDA (Food and Drug Administration) is an agency of the US federal government that is responsible for protecting and promoting public health through the regulation of food, drugs, medical devices, cosmetics, and other related products. The FDA ensures the safety, effectiveness, and quality of these products, and regulates their labeling, manufacturing, distribution, and marketing to ensure that they meet strict standards for safety and efficacy. The agency also conducts research, provides scientific expertise and advice, and works closely with other government agencies, healthcare professionals, industry, and consumers to promote public health and prevent harm from products under its jurisdiction.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: When what is FDA (Food and Drug Administration)  was drafted/ratified/approved

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The FDA (Food and Drug Administration) was not created by a single act of Congress or a specific constitutional amendment. Instead, it is the result of a process of evolution that began in the early 1900s. 

In 1906, the Pure Food and Drugs Act was passed by Congress, which prohibited the sale of misbranded or adulterated food and drugs in interstate commerce. This was followed by the creation of the Bureau of Chemistry, which was charged with enforcing the new law. 

In 1927, the Bureau of Chemistry was reorganized as the Food, Drug, and Insecticide Administration, which was later renamed the FDA in 1930. 

Over the decades, the FDA's role and responsibilities have expanded, and it operates today as a federal agency within the Department of Health and Human Services.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Presidential Decree No. 851 is a law that was passed in the Philippines in 1975. The law requires employers to grant their employees a 13th-month pay, which is equivalent to one twelfth (1/12) of the employee's basic salary earned within a calendar year. It is a form of monetary benefit granted to employees, usually given before Christmas or at the end of the year, as an additional financial assistance to help defray the cost of their basic needs and expenses. 

Under Presidential Decree No. 851, all employers regardless of their business or employment status, including government and agricultural employees, are mandated to pay their employees a 13th-month pay. The law also specifies that the 13th-month pay should not be less than 1/12 of the total basic salary that an employee has earned throughout the year and that it should be given no later than December 24 of each year. Employers who fail to comply with this law may be faced with legal penalties and sanctions.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: When was PRESIDENTIAL DECREE No. 851  drafted/ratified/approved

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: Presidential Decree No. 851 was drafted and approved by the former President of the Philippines, Ferdinand Marcos, on December 16, 1975. It was enacted as a means of providing additional financial assistance to employees to help them cope with the rising cost of living during that time.
